sanskrit_devanagari:
न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचि-
न्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः ।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ॥ २० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin
nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ |
ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṁ purāṇo
na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre || 20 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.020 = MBh_06,024.020). Body text identical. Verse is also recorded as Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.2.18.

translations.verbatim_quote: For the soul there is neither birth nor death at any time. He has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. He is unborn, eternal, ever-existing and primeval. He is not slain when the body is slain.

The soul is neither born, nor does it ever die; nor having once existed, does it ever cease to be. The soul is without birth, eternal, immortal, and ageless. It is not destroyed when the body is destroyed.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt, ~290 chars of 3935-char full purport)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Qualitatively, the small atomic fragmental part of the Supreme Spirit is one with the Supreme. He undergoes no changes like the body. Sometimes the soul is called the steady, or kūṭa-stha. The body is subject to six kinds of transformations.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Na kadacit, never; is ayam, this One; jayate, born i.e. the Self has no change in the form of being born — to which matter is subject –; va, and (– va is used in the sense of and); na mriyate, It never dies. By this is denied the final change in the form of destruction.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the four predicates (aja: unborn; nitya: eternal; śāśvata: permanent; purāṇa: ancient) as systematically denying the six modifications of matter (ṣaḍ-vikāra: birth, existence, growth, change, decay, death). 'Not now, not having come to be, not going to come to be, not again' rules out every temporal mode of becoming. The Self is older than what becomes, and is unaffected when the body that becomes is destroyed.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As the self is eternal for the reasons mentioned (above), and hence free from modifications, it is said that all the attributes of the insentient (body) like birth, death etc., never touch the self. In this connection, as the statement, 'It is never born, It never dies' is in the present tense.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the four predicates as ruling out the body's attributes (birth, growth, decay, death) from the embodied jīva. The use of the present tense ('is never born, never dies') is significant — it asserts that the eternality holds at every present moment, not merely at some abstract endpoint. purāṇa (ancient) is read literally: the jīva precedes the body; it is older than this birth and will outlast this death.

positions.rendering: ancient — predates this birth, outlasts this death

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: purāṇa literally means 'old / ancient' — etymologically connected to purā (long ago). Prabhupada's 'primeval' captures the prior-to-time sense; Mukundananda's 'ageless' modernises it into an attribute of timelessness. Both bhasyas read the term as a temporal predicate — the Self is older than every body it inhabits. The engineering layer's correct mapping is to durations longer than any single tenure (the customer-need that predates the company, the contract that predates the codebase).

positions.rendering: the four predicates systematically deny the six modifications of matter
the body's attributes (birth, growth, decay, death) never touch the embodied

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both bhasyas read the verse as a closed enumeration that exhausts the modes of becoming. Shankara's framing (ṣaḍ-vikāra) is more technical; Ramanuja's framing is more colloquial. The translators do not surface this; they let the four predicates stand without classification.

literal_meaning: This is never born, nor does it die at any time; it has not come to be, will not come to be, nor again. Unborn, eternal, ever-existing, ancient — it is not slain when the body is slain.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse — quoted from Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.2.18 — is the chapter's most sustained negation of becoming applied to the embodied. The four predicates (aja: unborn; nitya: eternal; śāśvata: permanent; purāṇa: ancient) systematically rule out the modes of temporal modification. 'Has not come to be, will not come to be, nor again' explicitly denies past, future, and recurrence. Both major commentarial traditions read the verse as the substrate-claim: the embodied is older than every body it has occupied and will outlast every body it will occupy. The verse closes the doctrinal block (2.16-2.20) by giving the imperishable substrate its full set of names.
